nativeRange.habitat: Coastal Atlantic-drainage streams and rivers in southeastern Brazil (states of São Paulo, Paraná, and Santa Catarina), plus some tributaries of the upper Paraná River basin; typically shallow, well-oxygenated, clear to slightly turbid water over sand, gravel, or rocky substrate with leaf litter and riparian vegetation

decorPreferences: leaf litter (dried oak or beech leaves)
caves or flat stones as territorial resting points for males

diet.notes: Benthic forager. Accepts high-quality sinking pellets, wafers, and catfish tablets as a staple. Relishes live and frozen foods: bloodworms, tubifex, daphnia, brine shrimp, white worms, and chopped earthworms. Will graze on biofilm and detritus. Wild diet includes worms, benthic crustaceans, insects, and plant matter. Feed after lights-out to ensure this species competes effectively with faster tankmates.

goodWith: other cool-water South American fish (small tetras, small characins)
Scleromystax barbatus (overlapping temperature range and biotope)
non-aggressive dwarf cichlids tolerating cooler temperatures
avoidWith: tropical species requiring temperatures above 26°C (incompatible thermal range)
fast, competitive feeders that outcompete for sinking foods

imageCandidates.notes: FishBase species image for Scleromystax macropterus. Verify availability and licensing before use.

summary: A cool-tolerant southeastern Brazilian armored catfish in which adult males develop a dramatically elongated dorsal fin — the "sailfin" feature. Reclassified from Corydoras to the sister-genus Scleromystax by Britto (2003). IUCN-listed as Endangered; rarely exported. Intermediate care due to cool-water requirement (14–22°C) and male territorial dynamics.
careNotes:
Scleromystax macropterus was reclassified from Corydoras into the resurrected genus Scleromystax by Britto in 2003, based on morphological and phylogenetic evidence — Scleromystax is the sister-genus to Corydoras within the subfamily Corydoradinae, sharing the broader tribe but distinguished by stronger sexual dimorphism and larger adult size. This species is closely related to the Barbatus Cory (S. barbatus, fw-fish-151). The common name "Sailfin" references the dramatically extended dorsal-fin rays that adult males develop at sexual maturity; the species epithet macropterus means "long-fin" in Greek. The type locality is Paranaguá, Paraná, Brazil.

The most critical husbandry factor is temperature: S. macropterus originates from subtropical southeastern Brazilian coastal drainages and requires cool, well-oxygenated water in the range 14–22°C. Sustained temperatures above 24°C cause stress, immune suppression, and shortened lifespan. Standard tropical aquarium heaters are typically inappropriate; an unheated, well-ventilated room-temperature setup suits temperate climates well, and a chiller may be needed in warmer regions. Good water movement and high dissolved oxygen are important — the species is a facultative air-breather but benefits from well-aerated conditions.

Fine sand substrate is essential: coarse gravel erodes the sensitive barbels, leading to bacterial infection. A substrate depth of at least 3 cm is recommended. Keep in groups of five or more to encourage natural shoaling behaviour and reduce stress. Males are somewhat territorial toward one another during the breeding season; provide adequate space (minimum 100 cm tank length) and visual breaks with décor to diffuse aggression. Pectoral and dorsal spines can inflict painful wounds — handle with care. Do not use copper-based medications; as with all Callichthyidae, the species is copper-sensitive. The IUCN lists this species as Endangered (EN, B2ab(iii), 2018) owing to habitat degradation and deforestation across its restricted Brazilian coastal range; wild-caught specimens are now rarely exported.
breedingNotes: Breeding S. macropterus in captivity is achievable but moderately demanding. Condition a group of two or more males and three or more females with protein-rich live and frozen foods for several weeks. Spawning is triggered by a partial water change using slightly cooler water (a 2–4°C drop), replicating seasonal rainfall and temperature shifts in the wild, combined with increased oxygenation. Eggs are deposited on plant leaves, the glass, or substrate. Adults will consume eggs if not separated — transfer eggs to a separate hatching container with gentle aeration and an anti-fungal agent (dilute methylene blue). Eggs hatch in approximately 3–5 days at 20°C. Fry accept micro-worms and freshly hatched Artemia nauplii from free-swimming stage. Females are fuller-bodied and lack the elongated dorsal fin of mature males, making sexing straightforward in adult fish.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Scleromystax macropterus
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided adult size (80–90 mm SL), temperature range (14–20°C), pH (5.0–7.0), hardness (18–143 ppm), native range (coastal São Paulo, Paraná, Santa Catarina Brazil and upper Paraná tributaries), biotope, diet, male territorial behaviour, breeding trigger (cool water change, increased oxygenation), egg incubation (~3–4 days), fry foods (microworm/Artemia nauplii), venomous pectoral spines, facultative air-breathing, and taxonomy note (Britto 2003 reclassification from Corydoras to Scleromystax).

additional.name: FishBase — Scleromystax macropterus
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (max 8.7 cm SL), temperature range (18–21°C), pH (6.0–8.0), hardness (2–25 dGH), native range (São Paulo to Santa Catarina Brazil and upper Paraná tributaries), taxonomy (Callichthyidae, Siluriformes, Corydoradinae), obligate air-breathing notation, and synonym Corydoras macropterus confirming pre-Britto classification.

additional.name: SeriouslyFish — Scleromystax barbatus
additional.notes: Cross-reference for genus-level Scleromystax husbandry parallels (cool-water requirement, fine-sand substrate, venomous pectoral spines, copper sensitivity, male odontode sexual dimorphism, group-keeping recommendations). S. barbatus is the type species of the genus and a close relative.

additional.name: Wikipedia — Sailfin corydoras
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (8.7 cm), common names (sailfin, bigfin, largefin corydoras), native range, IUCN Endangered status (EN B2ab(iii), assessed November 2018), commercial aquarium trade significance, original description as Corydoras macropterus (Regan, 1913), type locality Paranaguá Paraná Brazil, egg-scattering spawning in dense vegetation, and diet (worms, benthic crustaceans, insects, plant matter).

additional.name: ScotCat — Scleromystax macropterus
additional.notes: Supplementary source confirming diet (tablet, flake, frozen bloodworm, white worm, grindal worm), bristle/odontode development on cheeks of mature males, cool-water preference, native range restricted to coastal Brazilian drainages (São Paulo to Santa Catarina), and note that the species is becoming rare in the export trade.

fish.breedingNotes: Conditional spawning triggered by cooler water change (2–4°C drop) plus increased oxygenation after conditioning on live/frozen foods. Egg scatterer on plants and glass; adults will consume eggs — relocate eggs to a separate tank with gentle aeration for best yield. Eggs hatch in ~3–5 days. Fry accept micro-worms and brine shrimp nauplii.
